title: Lord of the Rings Journeys in Middle-Earth

walkthrough.title: Download the App
walkthrough.text: Before anything else, one player needs to download the free Journeys in Middle-earth companion app. This game is app-driven, meaning the app controls the story, enemy behavior, map reveals, and more. You can find it on the Amazon Appstore, Apple iOS App Store, Google Play, or Steam. Get it installed and opened up before you start unpacking the box.

walkthrough.title: Select Campaign and Difficulty
walkthrough.text: In the app, select New Game. The app will guide you through choosing a campaign, a difficulty, and a save slot. For your very first game, the rulebook recommends the Bones of Arnor campaign on Normal difficulty. This is a multi-adventure campaign, meaning each game session is one adventure in a larger story. The app saves your progress between sessions, so you do not have to finish everything in one sitting.

walkthrough.title: Create Party and Select Heroes
walkthrough.text: Each player selects a hero in the app. There are six heroes to choose from: Aragorn, Beravor, Bilbo, Elena, Gimli, and Legolas. When you pick a hero, find the matching plastic figure and hero card and place them in your play area. These heroes stay with you for the entire campaign. If you are playing solo, you control two heroes. The game supports one to five players.

walkthrough.title: Gather Starting Items
walkthrough.text: The app assigns starting items for each hero. Find the item cards that match the name and tier shown in the app and place them near your hero card. There are 83 item cards total in the box. Items include weapons like Swords, Daggers, Staffs, and Great Bows, as well as armor like Travel Garb. Each item card shows a stat icon in the upper-left corner and ability text. You can change starting items in the app if you prefer different gear.

walkthrough.title: Create Damage, Fear, and Weakness Decks
walkthrough.text: Find the 28 damage cards, 28 fear cards, and 20 weakness skill cards. Shuffle each group into its own separate face-down deck and place them in the center of the play area where everyone can reach. These decks are shared by all players. Damage cards represent physical injuries, fear cards represent psychological strain, and weakness cards clutter your skill deck with useless cards.

walkthrough.title: Create the Supply
walkthrough.text: Separate the following tokens into piles to form the supply: 30 exploration and inspiration tokens (they are double-sided), 20 search and threat tokens (also double-sided), 8 person tokens in four variations, and the 12 enemy banners with their plastic stands. Also separate the three types of boon cards: Hidden, Emboldened, and Determined. Set aside all 22 journey map tiles, 2 battle map tiles, and the 31 plastic figures (6 heroes and 25 enemies). For the first adventure, return the battle map tiles and terrain tokens to the box as they are not needed.

walkthrough.title: Choose Roles
walkthrough.text: Each player selects a role in the app. There are six roles, each with a different purpose. Burglar evades enemies and is self-sufficient. Captain helps the party prepare for any situation. Guardian protects others and has strong defenses. Hunter attacks for heavy damage. Musician inspires others to succeed at their tasks. Pathfinder helps the party move quickly. The recommended starting roles are: Aragorn as Captain, Beravor as Pathfinder, Bilbo as Burglar, Elena as Musician, Gimli as Guardian, and Legolas as Hunter. Roles are not permanent and can be changed between adventures.

walkthrough.title: Gather Skill Cards
walkthrough.text: Each hero gathers exactly 15 skill cards for their personal skill deck. These come from three sources plus one weakness. First, take one copy of each of the six basic skill cards numbered 1 through 6 with the word Basic on the bottom. Second, take the five skill cards numbered 1 through 5 with your hero's name on the bottom. Third, take the three skill cards numbered 1 through 3 with your chosen role on the bottom. These role cards have green-colored text boxes for easy identification. Finally, take one random weakness card from the top of the weakness deck. That gives you 6 basic plus 5 hero plus 3 role plus 1 weakness, totaling 15 cards.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Starting Skill and Create Skill Decks
walkthrough.text: Find your role's starting skill card. This is the card with the number 1 after the role name at the bottom and stars next to the card name. For example, if Beravor is a Pathfinder, her starting skill is Trailblazer (Pathfinder 1). Place this card face-up below your hero card. This is your first prepared card. Then, shuffle the remaining 14 cards together to form your personal skill deck and place it face-down in your play area. Each player now has their own deck.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Tiles and Tokens
walkthrough.text: In the app, select Embark and follow the instructions. The app will tell you which journey map tiles to place and where to put tokens. Each map tile has a unique alphanumeric code printed on it to help you find and orient it correctly. Leave extra room around the edges for new tiles that will be placed during the adventure. The app shows fog around the starting tiles to indicate roughly where the map will expand. After following all the app instructions, place your hero figures on the map as directed. You are ready to begin your adventure.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: A game of Journeys in Middle-earth is played over a series of rounds. Each round has three phases that you resolve in order. First is the Action Phase, where heroes move around the map, interact with the world, and attack enemies. Second is the Shadow Phase, where enemies move and attack, darkness strikes, and threat increases. Third is the Rally Phase, where heroes prepare for the next round by resetting their skill decks and scouting new cards. After the rally phase, a new round begins with the action phase. This cycle continues until the adventure ends.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase: Your Turn
walkthrough.text: During the action phase, each hero takes a turn. The heroes choose the order they take turns, and this order can change from round to round. On your turn, you perform two actions. You can pick the same action twice or two different actions. The three actions are Travel, Attack, and Interact. After all heroes have taken a turn, someone selects the hourglass button in the lower-right corner of the app to proceed to the shadow phase.

walkthrough.title: Travel Action
walkthrough.text: The travel action lets your hero move around the game map. The map is made up of map tiles divided into spaces, which are areas enclosed by gray borders. Spaces that share a gray border are adjacent. When you travel, you can move twice, and each move takes you to one adjacent space. You can interrupt your two moves to perform your second action in between. For example, move one space, perform an interact action, then move one more space.

walkthrough.title: Exploration
walkthrough.text: Each journey map tile has a square box where an exploration token may be placed. A tile with an exploration token on it is unexplored. When your hero moves into any space on an unexplored tile, you must immediately explore it. To explore, select the exploration token in the app and then select Confirm. The app instructs you to discard the exploration token and gain one inspiration. Simply flip the exploration token over to its inspiration side and place it on your hero card. Exploring is not a separate action. It happens automatically when you enter an unexplored tile.

walkthrough.title: Attack Action
walkthrough.text: The attack action lets you fight an enemy group. An enemy group is one or more plastic figures of the same type that move and attack together. To attack, choose an item card that has a stat icon in its upper-left corner, and choose an enemy group in range. An enemy in your space is always in range. An enemy in an adjacent space is in range only if your item has the ranged icon. Then you perform an attack test using one of the stats listed on the item card. You spend your successes to resolve the item's abilities, such as applying hits to the enemy. Each ability can only be resolved once per attack.

walkthrough.title: Interact Action
walkthrough.text: The interact action lets you encounter points of interest on the map, indicated by search tokens, person tokens, and threat tokens. If your hero is in the same space as one of these tokens, you can interact with it by selecting the token in the app and then selecting the button with the action icon. The app then provides instructions to follow. Interacting with tokens is how heroes progress the adventure, discover items, gain experience, and neutralize threats. You can inspect any token at any time without spending an action by selecting it in the app and reading its description, then selecting Cancel.

walkthrough.title: How Tests Work
walkthrough.text: Tests are the primary way heroes interact with the world and fight enemies. Each test uses one of your five stats: Might, Wisdom, Agility, Spirit, or Wit. To perform a test, reveal cards from the top of your skill deck equal to your stat value. Count the success icons in the upper-left corners of the revealed cards. Ignore all card text during tests. Some tests require you to input your success count into the app. Others give a target number you need to meet or exceed to pass. After the test, all revealed cards go to your discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Fate and Inspiration
walkthrough.text: Your revealed cards during a test may also show fate icons. Fate icons have no effect on their own, but you can spend inspiration tokens to convert them into successes. Each inspiration spent converts one fate icon to one success. To spend inspiration, take a token from your hero card and return it to the supply. Heroes gain inspiration from exploring tiles, from certain skill card effects, and from other game events. Your hero card shows an inspiration limit, which is the maximum number of inspiration tokens you can hold at once.

walkthrough.title: Attack Tests and Applying Hits
walkthrough.text: When you attack, you test a stat from your weapon's item card. Then you spend successes to activate the weapon's abilities. Most abilities add hits to the enemy. Select the enemy portrait in the app, go to the attack tab, and use the plus button to input hits. Some abilities also add attack modifiers like pierce, smite, sunder, cleave, lethal, or stun. Select those modifiers in the app. After inputting everything, select Apply. If an enemy has armor or sorcery, hits reduce those first. If the enemy's health drops to zero, it is defeated and removed from the board. If not defeated, the enemy regains all armor and sorcery and may counterattack.

walkthrough.title: Shadow Phase: Enemy Activation
walkthrough.text: During the enemy activation step, each enemy group attempts to move and attack. The app assigns each enemy a target hero. The enemy moves up to its indicated number of spaces toward that hero along the shortest path and stops when in range. If it cannot reach its target but can reach a closer hero, that hero becomes the new target. If the enemy is in range, select Attack and the app shows the damage and fear the hero might suffer. If the enemy cannot reach any hero, select No Target and the enemy gets a new instruction, usually moving closer.

walkthrough.title: Shadow Phase: Darkness and Threat
walkthrough.text: After enemies activate, the darkness step occurs if the app instructs it. A hero is in darkness if they are in a space with a printed darkness icon, in a space with a darkness token, or if the app states they are in darkness. Each hero in darkness suffers the fear amount indicated by the app. Then comes the threat step. Threat is tracked by a bar at the top of the app screen. It increases each round by two per hero playing, one per threat token on the map, and one per unexplored tile. When threat reaches a threshold, a threat event activates. If the threat bar fills completely, the heroes lose the adventure.

walkthrough.title: Rally Phase
walkthrough.text: After the shadow phase, the rally phase begins. First, each hero resets their skill deck by shuffling their discard pile into their remaining deck cards. Prepared cards are never shuffled in. Then, the app instructs heroes to Scout 2. You reveal the top two cards of your skill deck. You may prepare one by placing it face-up below your hero card. You can have a maximum of four prepared cards at any time. Cards you do not prepare go on the top or bottom of your deck in any order. Place useful cards on top and useless ones like weaknesses on the bottom. After scouting, select Continue to begin a new round.

walkthrough.title: Damage and Fear
walkthrough.text: When your hero suffers damage or fear, reveal a card from the corresponding deck and place it face-up in your play area. Resolve its effect. Many cards instruct you to flip face-down or discard. If a card says keep it face-up, it has a persistent negative effect. If you suffer multiple damage or fear at once, reveal them one at a time, resolving each before the next. Sometimes you are granted a chance to negate damage or fear by testing a stat. Each success prevents one point of damage or fear. Your hero card shows damage and fear limits. If your total damage or fear (face-up and face-down combined) reaches the limit, you must perform a last stand.

walkthrough.title: Last Stands and Defeat
walkthrough.text: When you hit your damage or fear limit, select the party button in the lower-left corner of the app, select your hero portrait, and select which limit was reached. The app provides a last stand test. If you fail, your hero is defeated and removed from the board. The remaining heroes continue playing, but if they do not complete the final objective by the start of the next shadow phase, the adventure is lost. If you pass the last stand, you follow the app instructions and continue playing. If you hit both limits at once, you perform two last stands in the order of your choice.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing Adventures
walkthrough.text: Each adventure has a series of objectives displayed beneath the threat bar in the app. Complete one objective and the app gives you the next. If the heroes complete the final objective before the threat bar fills, they win the adventure. Winning provides greater rewards. However, whether you win or lose, all heroes proceed to the next adventure of the campaign. The campaign reaches a climactic conclusion after several adventures. The same adventure can vary each time you play it because the app generates different map layouts, enemies, events, and challenges from many possibilities.

walkthrough.title: Provoking Enemies
walkthrough.text: When your hero is in the same space as a readied enemy group, certain actions provoke that enemy, causing it to attack. You provoke an enemy before moving out of its space and before interacting with a token in its space. To provoke, select the provoke button to the left of the enemy portraits in the app, then select the enemy. It resolves an attack against your hero. After the attack, you can proceed with your move or interact action. Unlike regular activations or counterattacks, provoking does not exhaust the enemy. Enemies become exhausted after the app activates them or after they counterattack, and exhausted enemies cannot be provoked.

walkthrough.title: Prepared Skill Cards and Keywords
walkthrough.text: Prepared skill cards sit face-up below your hero card. Their abilities are only active while prepared. Most abilities describe when they trigger and many instruct you to discard the card to use them. Skill cards contain bolded keywords that are shorthand for common abilities. Sprint X lets you discard the card during your turn to move X extra spaces. Strike X lets you discard during an attack to add X hits. Guard X lets you discard when you or a hero in your space would suffer damage or fear to prevent X total. Rest X lets you discard at end of turn to remove X face-down damage or fear cards. Hide lets you discard after a test to gain a Hidden boon card. Scout X lets you reveal X cards and prepare one.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Fellowship
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first few rounds of the Bones of Arnor campaign with three heroes: Aragorn as Captain, Beravor as Pathfinder, and Legolas as Hunter. Three players is the recommended count. If you have more or fewer players, you can still follow along to learn the flow. The app will generate the map and objectives, but we will focus on the core decisions and common mistakes. Ready? Let's embark.

walkthrough.title: Setup Recap
walkthrough.text: Everyone should have their hero card, plastic figure, starting items, and a 15-card skill deck. Aragorn has his Captain starting skill prepared below his hero card. Beravor has Trailblazer (Pathfinder 1) prepared. Legolas has his Hunter starting skill prepared. The damage, fear, and weakness decks are shuffled and central. The supply of tokens and boon cards is organized. The app has been set to the Bones of Arnor campaign on Normal difficulty, and you have placed the starting map tiles and tokens as directed. Hero figures are on the map.

walkthrough.title: Round 1: Choosing Turn Order
walkthrough.text: The action phase begins. Remember, heroes choose their own turn order each round. This is a cooperative game, so talk it out. Look at the map and decide who should go first based on position and objectives. For this tutorial, let's have Beravor go first because she is a Pathfinder and can move efficiently to explore. Aragorn goes second, and Legolas goes third.

walkthrough.title: Beravor's Turn: Travel and Explore
walkthrough.text: Beravor takes the Travel action as her first action. She moves two spaces toward an unexplored tile. When she enters the unexplored tile, exploration triggers automatically. She selects the exploration token in the app and confirms. The app tells her to discard the token and gain one inspiration. She flips the token to its inspiration side and places it on her hero card. Beravor's Trailblazer ability also fires: before she sprints, she gains one inspiration. The app may reveal new map tiles and tokens. For her second action, Beravor uses Travel again to keep moving deeper into the map. Good scouting.

walkthrough.title: Why Exploring Early Matters
walkthrough.text: Exploring tiles early is critical for two reasons. First, you gain inspiration, which lets you convert fate icons to successes on future tests. Second, each unexplored tile adds one to the threat increase each round. The faster you explore, the slower threat climbs. With three heroes, threat already increases by six per threat step just from having three players. Every unexplored tile and threat token makes it worse. Early exploration keeps the threat bar manageable.

walkthrough.title: Aragorn's Turn: Travel and Interact
walkthrough.text: Aragorn uses his first action to Travel, moving two spaces toward a search token the app placed on the map. For his second action, he performs an Interact action on the search token. He selects the token in the app and selects the action button. The app instructs him to test a stat. Aragorn reveals cards from his skill deck equal to that stat value, counts successes, and inputs the result. The app resolves the outcome, perhaps granting him a new item or lore.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Legolas Forgets to Check for Provoke
walkthrough.text: Legolas has an enemy group of Orcs in his space from a previous event. He wants to move away to interact with a search token on another tile. He announces he is using the Travel action and starts to move. Stop! There is a readied enemy group in his space. Before Legolas can move out, he must provoke that enemy. Provoking means the Orc group gets a free attack against him. In this tutorial, the Orc group attacks for two damage and one fear. Legolas has to test might to try to negate. He only gets one success, so he prevents one damage but suffers one damage and one fear. He reveals those cards and resolves their effects. Then he can move.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check for Provoke
walkthrough.text: Forgetting to provoke enemies is one of the most common mistakes new players make. Before you move out of a space with a readied enemy, or before you interact with a token in a space with a readied enemy, you must provoke. Look at the enemy portraits in the app. If any enemy in your space is readied (not darkened), it will attack you first. Plan your actions accordingly. Sometimes it is better to attack the enemy first to exhaust it, then move or interact safely on your second action.

walkthrough.title: Ending the Action Phase
walkthrough.text: All three heroes have taken their turns. Beravor explored and moved deep into the map. Aragorn investigated a search token. Legolas dealt with the provoke and then moved to a better position. Now, one player selects the hourglass button in the lower-right corner of the app to end the action phase and begin the shadow phase.

walkthrough.title: Shadow Phase: Enemies Activate
walkthrough.text: The app activates each enemy group one at a time. The Orc group is told to move toward and attack a hero. It moves along the shortest path and stops when in range. If it reaches a hero, select Attack in the app. The app shows the damage and fear values. The targeted hero may test a stat to negate some of it. After the enemy attacks, it becomes exhausted and its portrait darkens in the app. Exhausted enemies cannot counterattack or be provoked until the app readies them at the end of the shadow phase.

walkthrough.title: Shadow Phase: Threat Increases
walkthrough.text: After enemy activations and any darkness effects, the threat step occurs. With three heroes, threat increases by six just for having three players, plus one for each threat token on the map and one for each unexplored tile. Watch the threat bar in the app carefully. If it reaches a threshold number, a threat event fires and the heroes must resolve its instructions. If the bar fills completely, the adventure is lost. This is why exploring early and removing threat tokens through interact actions is so important.

walkthrough.title: Rally Phase: Reset and Scout
walkthrough.text: Now each hero resets their skill deck by shuffling their discard pile back into their remaining deck. Prepared cards stay where they are. Then the app instructs everyone to Scout 2. Each hero reveals the top two cards of their deck. You may prepare one of them by placing it face-up below your hero card, as long as you do not exceed four prepared cards. Place any card you do not prepare on the top or bottom of your deck. Put useful cards on top for your next tests and weaknesses on the bottom.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Legolas Wastes Inspiration
walkthrough.text: Legolas has three inspiration tokens and his hero card shows an inspiration limit of five. During the next action phase, he explores a tile and would gain one inspiration. But then he attacks an Orc group. He reveals cards and gets one success and two fate icons. He needs at least three successes for a good result. Legolas decides to spend all three of his remaining inspiration to convert all fate icons, but he only has two fate icons. The third inspiration is wasted because there is no fate icon to convert. You can only convert fate icons you actually revealed. You cannot create successes from nothing.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spend Inspiration Wisely
walkthrough.text: Inspiration is a precious resource. Each token converts exactly one fate icon to one success. You cannot spend more inspiration than you have fate icons showing. Before spending, count your fate icons and decide how many you actually need to convert. Sometimes it is better to save inspiration for a more critical test later, like negating damage from a powerful enemy or passing a key interact test. Beravor's hero ability lets her scout after sprinting, hiding, or striking, which helps her see good cards. Aragorn's Captain skills can help allies prepare. Use each hero's strengths to generate and conserve inspiration across the party.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Prepared Cards
walkthrough.text: In Round 3, Aragorn has scouted twice and has several prepared cards below his hero card. But he forgets that his prepared Strike 2 card exists. He attacks an enemy and inputs his hits, missing by just two hits from defeating the Orc. If he had remembered his prepared Strike 2 card, he could have discarded it during the attack to add two extra hits, which would have been enough to finish the enemy. Instead, the undefeated enemy counterattacks him for damage and fear. That is a painful mistake when the answer was sitting right in front of him.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Review Your Prepared Cards
walkthrough.text: Before every action, glance at your prepared cards. They are your special abilities for this adventure. Strike cards add hits during attacks. Guard cards prevent damage and fear when you or a nearby hero is attacked. Sprint cards let you move extra spaces. Rest cards remove face-down damage and fear at end of turn. Hide cards give you the Hidden boon after a test. These keywords are your lifeline. A prepared card sitting unused is a wasted resource. Make reviewing your prepared cards a habit before every action.

walkthrough.title: Continue the Adventure
walkthrough.text: Play continues round by round. Each round follows the same structure: action phase where heroes take turns performing two actions each, shadow phase where enemies activate and threat increases, and rally phase where you reset decks and scout. Keep exploring tiles to gain inspiration and reduce threat buildup. Interact with search and person tokens to progress objectives. Attack enemies when they block your path or when the app presents opportunities. Coordinate with your fellow heroes on turn order and positioning. The adventure ends when you complete the final objective or the threat bar fills.

walkthrough.title: Completing the Adventure
walkthrough.text: You have seen all three phases, all three actions, and three common mistakes. Whether your fellowship wins or loses this adventure, the campaign continues. After the adventure, the app lets you spend lore and experience to upgrade items and purchase new skill cards. You can also change roles between adventures. The Bones of Arnor campaign spans several adventures, and the story adapts based on your choices. Each replay generates different maps, encounters, and events.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations, Fellowship
walkthrough.text: You have learned how to set up the game, take turns, explore the map, attack enemies, interact with tokens, and manage the shadow phase. You have seen Legolas forget to provoke and waste inspiration, and Aragorn neglect his prepared cards. Remember: always check for provoke before moving or interacting, spend inspiration only on actual fate icons, and review your prepared cards before every action.

walkthrough.title: Before You Journey On
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive deeper into Middle-earth, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy advice on exploration pacing, party composition, and threat management.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every hero, role, keyword, attack modifier, and game term.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. May your journey be blessed by the light of Earendil!

summary.title: Welcome to the Fellowship
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 heroes: Aragorn (Captain), Beravor (Pathfinder), Legolas (Hunter)
Follow along even with more or fewer players
Covers first few rounds of the Bones of Arnor campaign

summary.title: Setup Recap
bullets: Each hero has hero card, figure, starting items, and 15-card skill deck
Starting skill card prepared below each hero card
Damage, fear, weakness decks shuffled centrally; supply organized
Starting map tiles and tokens placed per app

summary.title: Round 1: Choosing Turn Order
bullets: Heroes choose turn order cooperatively each round
Consider position and objectives when deciding order
Example: Pathfinder goes first for efficient exploration

summary.title: Beravor's Turn: Travel and Explore
bullets: Travel action: move 2 spaces toward an unexplored tile
Exploration triggers automatically; gain 1 inspiration
Trailblazer ability grants bonus inspiration before sprinting
Second action: Travel again to push deeper into the map

summary.title: Why Exploring Early Matters
bullets: Exploration gives inspiration for converting fate to successes
Each unexplored tile adds +1 to threat increase per round
With 3 heroes: base threat increase is +6 per round before tokens and tiles
Fast exploration keeps threat bar manageable

summary.title: Aragorn's Turn: Travel and Interact
bullets: Travel to reach a search token, then Interact with it
Select token in app, select action button, test the indicated stat
Input successes; app resolves outcome (items, lore, story progress)

summary.title: The Mistake: Legolas Forgets to Check for Provoke
bullets: Legolas tries to move out of a space with a readied Orc group
Must provoke first: enemy gets a free attack before he can leave
Orc attacks for 2 damage and 1 fear; Legolas tests might to negate
Suffers damage and fear that could have been avoided

summary.title: Lesson: Always Check for Provoke
bullets: Before moving out of or interacting in a readied enemy's space, you must provoke
Readied enemies have bright portraits; exhausted are darkened
Strategy: attack enemy first to exhaust it, then move/interact safely

summary.title: Ending the Action Phase
bullets: All heroes have taken turns with 2 actions each
Select hourglass button in app to start shadow phase

summary.title: Shadow Phase: Enemies Activate
bullets: App activates each enemy group to move toward and attack a target hero
Enemies take shortest path, stop when in range
After attacking, enemy becomes exhausted (portrait darkens)
Exhausted enemies cannot counterattack or be provoked until readied

summary.title: Shadow Phase: Threat Increases
bullets: Threat increases: +2 per hero, +1 per threat token, +1 per unexplored tile
Threshold events fire when threat reaches marked levels
Threat bar full = adventure lost
Explore and remove threat tokens to slow threat buildup

summary.title: Rally Phase: Reset and Scout
bullets: Shuffle discard pile into remaining deck (prepared cards stay out)
Scout 2: reveal top 2 cards, may prepare 1 (max 4 prepared cards)
Place unprepared cards on top or bottom of deck strategically

summary.title: The Mistake: Legolas Wastes Inspiration
bullets: Legolas spends 3 inspiration but only has 2 fate icons showing
Third inspiration is wasted with nothing to convert
Inspiration only converts actual fate icons, not thin air

summary.title: Lesson: Spend Inspiration Wisely
bullets: Count fate icons before spending inspiration
Cannot spend more inspiration than fate icons revealed
Save inspiration for critical tests (negating damage, key interactions)
Use hero abilities to generate and conserve inspiration across the party

summary.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Prepared Cards
bullets: Aragorn forgets his prepared Strike 2 card during an attack
Misses defeating an enemy by exactly 2 hits
Undefeated enemy counterattacks for avoidable damage and fear

summary.title: Lesson: Review Your Prepared Cards
bullets: Before every action, check your prepared cards for usable keywords
Strike adds hits, Guard prevents damage/fear, Sprint moves extra, Rest heals
A prepared card sitting unused is a wasted resource

summary.title: Continue the Adventure
bullets: Rounds repeat: Action Phase, Shadow Phase, Rally Phase
Explore tiles, interact with tokens, attack enemies, coordinate with party
Adventure ends when final objective completed or threat bar fills

summary.title: Completing the Adventure
bullets: Campaign continues win or lose
After adventure: upgrade items and skills with lore and experience
Can change roles between adventures
Each replay generates different maps and encounters

summary.title: Congratulations, Fellowship
bullets: Practiced all 3 actions and all 3 phases
Watched Legolas forget to provoke and waste inspiration
Watched Aragorn neglect prepared cards
Remember: provoke check, count fate icons, review prepared cards

summary.title: Before You Journey On
bullets: Check out General Tips for exploration pacing and threat management strategy
Reference the Appendix for quick-reference on heroes, roles, keywords, and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide in the Q&A tab for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, forgetting to provoke enemies before moving out of their space or interacting in their space. This costs you surprise damage and fear. Second, ignoring the threat bar until it is too late. Threat increases every round and unexplored tiles make it worse. Third, hoarding inspiration instead of spending it on critical tests. Fourth, not reviewing prepared cards before acting, missing free hits, guards, or sprints that would change the outcome. Fifth, spreading out too much on the map so heroes cannot support each other. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest traps.

walkthrough.title: Explore Early and Often
walkthrough.text: Exploration is your best friend in Journeys in Middle-earth. Every unexplored tile adds one to the threat increase each round, and with three heroes that is already a base of six per round. Leaving three tiles unexplored means threat jumps by nine or more each round. That snowballs fast. Designate at least one hero, ideally the Pathfinder, as your primary explorer. Have them travel aggressively in the first few rounds to flip exploration tokens. You gain inspiration each time you explore, which fuels better tests for the whole party. Explore first, investigate later.

walkthrough.title: Manage Threat Actively
walkthrough.text: The threat bar is your game clock. It fills based on the number of heroes, threat tokens on the map, and unexplored tiles. You can reduce threat buildup by exploring tiles and interacting with threat tokens to remove them. Always prioritize removing threat tokens when you can. If you see the bar approaching a threshold, consider whether you can afford to trigger that event or if you need to push harder on exploration. Some threat events are manageable; others can end your adventure. The app lets you preview threshold events by selecting them, so check what is coming.

walkthrough.title: Coordinate Hero Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Turn order matters more than you might think. The heroes choose their own order each round, so use this to your advantage. Have the hero who plans to explore go first, since revealing new map tiles may open up interact opportunities for later heroes. Have the hero with Guard prepared cards go last so they can be in position to protect others during the shadow phase. If an enemy is blocking a token, have the attacker go before the interactor. Talk through your plan at the start of each action phase.

walkthrough.title: Stack Your Deck During Rally
walkthrough.text: The rally phase is more powerful than it looks. When you Scout 2, you see two cards and can prepare one. But the real power is in where you place the card you do not prepare. If it has success icons, put it on top of your deck so your next test draws it. If it is a weakness or a card with no icons, bury it on the bottom. Over several rounds, this deck stacking compounds. You will start drawing more successes and fewer blanks. Combined with prepared cards that let you look at or rearrange your deck, you can engineer reliable test outcomes.

walkthrough.title: Balance Attack and Avoidance
walkthrough.text: New players often try to fight every enemy they see. Enemies in Journeys in Middle-earth are tough, and even a successful attack can leave you with fewer cards in your deck for future tests. Sometimes it is better to let an enemy exhaust by taking its activation hit during the shadow phase and then move past it, rather than spending both your actions attacking. On the other hand, if an enemy is parked on an objective token you need to interact with, you have to deal with it. Use your Hunter to focus on combat and let other roles avoid it when possible.

walkthrough.title: Use Boon Cards Strategically
walkthrough.text: The three boon types are powerful but situational. Hidden prevents all damage and fear from one attack, then the boon is discarded. Use it when you expect to be targeted during the shadow phase or when you need to provoke an enemy. Emboldened discards itself at end of turn if no enemies are nearby, but lets you add two hits to an attack. Use it right before attacking. Determined lets you discard any number of revealed cards during a test and reveal that many replacements. Save it for a critical test. Do not waste boons on minor tests when a harder challenge is coming.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Hero's Strengths
walkthrough.text: Each hero has different stat distributions that make them better at certain tasks. Aragorn is balanced with Might 3 and Wisdom 4, making him a solid attacker and leader. Beravor has all 3s except Wit 2, making her versatile. Bilbo has Wit 4 and Agility 3 but low Might 2, so he excels at interact tests and evasion. Elena has Spirit 4 and Wisdom 3, making her great at support and some attacks. Gimli has the highest combined damage capacity with high Might. Legolas has Agility 4 and Wit 3, perfect for ranged attacks and nimble movement. Play to your hero's stats and let the party cover each other's weaknesses.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't forget to provoke enemies before moving/interacting in their space
Don't ignore the threat bar -- it is your game clock
Don't hoard inspiration -- spend it on critical tests
Don't ignore prepared cards -- they are free abilities
Don't spread heroes too far apart -- stay close enough to support each other

summary.title: Explore Early and Often
bullets: Each unexplored tile adds +1 to threat per round
Designate a primary explorer (ideally Pathfinder)
Exploring grants inspiration for better future tests
Explore first, investigate later

summary.title: Manage Threat Actively
bullets: Threat increases: +2 per hero, +1 per threat token, +1 per unexplored tile
Remove threat tokens via interact actions when possible
Preview upcoming threshold events by selecting them in the app
Threat bar full = adventure lost

summary.title: Coordinate Hero Turn Order
bullets: Explorer goes first to reveal new map tiles for others
Attacker goes before interactor if enemy blocks a token
Guardian goes last to be in position for shadow phase
Discuss the plan at the start of each action phase

summary.title: Stack Your Deck During Rally
bullets: Scout 2: prepare 1 card, place the other on top or bottom of deck
Success-icon cards go on top for next test; weaknesses go on bottom
Deck stacking compounds over rounds for more reliable tests
Combine with abilities that look at or rearrange your deck

summary.title: Balance Attack and Avoidance
bullets: Attacking uses cards from your deck, weakening future tests
Sometimes better to take a shadow phase hit and move past an enemy
Must fight enemies blocking objective tokens
Let the Hunter handle combat; others avoid when possible

summary.title: Use Boon Cards Strategically
bullets: Hidden: prevents all damage/fear from one attack (use for provoke or shadow phase)
Emboldened: adds 2 hits to your next attack (use right before attacking)
Determined: redraw cards during a test (save for critical tests)
Don't waste boons on minor tests when harder challenges are coming

summary.title: Know Your Hero's Strengths
bullets: Aragorn: Might 3, Wisdom 4 -- balanced attacker and leader
Beravor: Might 3, Wisdom 3, Agility 3, Spirit 3, Wit 2 -- versatile
Bilbo: Wit 4, Agility 3, low Might 2 -- excels at interact and evasion
Elena: Spirit 4, Wisdom 3 -- strong support
Gimli: high Might -- damage-focused tank
Legolas: Agility 4, Wit 3 -- ranged attacks and nimble movement

walkthrough.title: Deck Engineering
walkthrough.text: Your 15-card skill deck is your most important resource. Every test draws from it, and every card matters. Advanced players treat the rally phase as a deck-building opportunity. When you Scout 2, you are shaping your next round's performance. Place cards with success icons on top for your first test of the round. Bury weaknesses on the bottom. Over multiple rounds, you can engineer a deck where almost every draw contributes successes or fate icons. Combined with prepared cards that let you look at the top card before tests, like Elena's Elven Light, you can approach near-guaranteed outcomes on critical tests.

walkthrough.title: Inspiration Economy
walkthrough.text: Inspiration is the most flexible resource in the game. It converts fate to successes during any test, and some hero abilities let you spend or gain inspiration for additional effects. The cap on inspiration varies by hero (Aragorn has 4, Bilbo has 6, others vary). In a well-coordinated team, the heroes who explore gain inspiration and pass it to those who need it for combat or critical interactions. Elena's hero ability lets her share inspiration with nearby heroes. Aragorn's Captain skills can help the whole party prepare. Think of inspiration as a shared party resource, not an individual one.

walkthrough.title: Threat Management Calculus
walkthrough.text: The math of threat is deterministic. Each round, threat increases by two per hero plus one per threat token plus one per unexplored tile. With three heroes and two unexplored tiles, that is eight threat per round. A typical threat bar has several thresholds, with the final one ending the game. You can calculate exactly how many rounds you have before disaster strikes. Use this knowledge to pace your adventure. If you have ten rounds before the bar fills, plan your exploration, combat, and objective completion accordingly. Removing one threat token saves you one threat per round for the rest of the adventure, which compounds over time.

walkthrough.title: Role Synergies
walkthrough.text: The six roles synergize in specific ways. Captain plus Hunter is a strong combat core because the Captain can help prepare the party while the Hunter deals maximum damage. Pathfinder plus Musician creates an efficient exploration and support duo. Guardian plus any squishy hero like Bilbo or Elena provides protection. For a three-player party, Captain, Pathfinder, and Hunter cover the three pillars of preparation, mobility, and combat. For a larger party, add a Guardian for defense and a Musician for test support. Role selection is not permanent, so adjust between adventures based on what you struggled with.

walkthrough.title: Combat Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Combat in Journeys in Middle-earth is expensive. Every card you reveal for an attack test is a card that cannot contribute to your next test. Efficient combat means maximizing hits per test. Dual wielding is powerful: if you have two items that share a stat, you can attack with both and spend successes on abilities from either. The Strike keyword adds hits without needing more successes. Attack modifiers like pierce (ignores armor), smite (ignores sorcery), and cleave (full hits to all figures in a group) multiply your damage. Plan attacks to defeat enemies in one round rather than chipping away over multiple rounds, because undefeated enemies counterattack and regain armor and sorcery.

walkthrough.title: Damage and Fear Management
walkthrough.text: Your hero's damage and fear limits are your health pools. Beravor has damage 5 and fear 5, meaning she can take 4 of each before triggering a last stand on the fifth. Face-down damage and fear are better than face-up because face-up cards have persistent negative effects. The Rest keyword lets you discard face-down cards at end of turn, effectively healing. Guard cards prevent damage and fear before they happen, which is strictly better than healing after. Prioritize Guard during dangerous rounds and Rest during quieter ones. If a hero is close to their limit, have them hang back while others engage enemies.

walkthrough.title: Campaign Progression
walkthrough.text: Between adventures, you can spend lore to upgrade item cards to higher tiers and spend experience to purchase new skill cards for your deck. This is where long-term strategy matters. Upgrading a weapon to tier 2 or 3 gives it stronger abilities and more hits per success. New skill cards replace basic cards in your deck, improving the density of success and fate icons. Focus upgrades on your primary role function: Hunters should upgrade weapons, Guardians should upgrade armor, Pathfinders should upgrade movement items. Do not spread upgrades too thin across items you rarely use.

walkthrough.title: Positional Play
walkthrough.text: Advanced players pay careful attention to positioning on the map. Nearby means same space or adjacent space, and many abilities and effects use this keyword. A Guardian with Guard prepared cards should stay adjacent to the party's most vulnerable hero. A Hunter with a ranged weapon can attack from an adjacent space without provoking. Heroes in the same space share access to boon cards and support abilities. Before ending your turn, think about where enemies will likely move during the shadow phase and position yourself to minimize damage. Sometimes using your second action to reposition is better than performing a less impactful interaction.

walkthrough.title: Reading the App
walkthrough.text: The app gives more information than you might realize. You can select any threshold on the threat bar to preview its event. You can select enemy portraits to see their health, armor, sorcery, and any bonuses (especially for elite enemies). You can inspect tokens on the map by selecting them and reading descriptions without spending an action. Elite enemy groups have spiked portrait frames and banner icons, and all their bonuses are listed in the enemy menu. Use all of this information to plan your actions. Knowledge is power in Middle-earth.

walkthrough.title: Adapting to the Scenario
walkthrough.text: The app generates different content each time you play the same adventure. Map layouts, enemy placement, and events vary. This means rigid plans will not always work. Adaptive play is key. If the map generates a compact layout with lots of threat tokens, prioritize interact actions on threats. If it generates a sprawling layout with many unexplored tiles, prioritize exploration. If the app spawns elite enemies early, focus combat resources on them because elite enemies can counterattack even when exhausted and have powerful bonuses. Read the situation and respond accordingly rather than executing the same plan every time.

summary.title: Deck Engineering
bullets: Scout 2 each rally phase to shape your deck
Place success-icon cards on top, weaknesses on bottom
Over multiple rounds, build a deck that almost always produces successes
Combine with abilities like Elena's Elven Light to preview top card

summary.title: Inspiration Economy
bullets: Inspiration cap varies by hero (Aragorn: 4, Bilbo: 6, etc.)
Explorers generate inspiration; share it with heroes who need it
Elena's hero ability passes inspiration to nearby heroes
Treat inspiration as a shared party resource, not individual

bullets: Threat is deterministic: +2 per hero + 1 per threat token + 1 per unexplored tile
Calculate remaining rounds before threat bar fills
Removing 1 threat token saves 1 threat per round (compounds over time)
Pace exploration, combat, and objectives around the math

3-player ideal: Captain, Pathfinder, Hunter; adjust between adventures

entries.definition: Human hero. Might 3, Wisdom 4, Agility 2, Spirit 2, Wit 3. Damage limit 5, Fear limit 5. Inspiration limit 4. Recommended starting role: Captain. His hero ability triggers when heroes scout during the rally phase, allowing him and nearby heroes to each reveal one additional card.

Human hero. Might 3, Wisdom 3, Agility 3, Spirit 3, Wit 2. Damage limit 5, Fear limit 5. Inspiration limit 4. Recommended starting role: Pathfinder. Once per round, after she sprints, hides, or strikes, she may scout 1.

Hobbit hero. Might 2, Wisdom 2, Agility 3, Spirit 3, Wit 4. Damage limit 3, Fear limit 7. Inspiration limit 6. Recommended starting role: Burglar. Before he interacts, he looks at the top card of his deck, and if it has a success icon, he gains 1 inspiration. Can equip only 1 armor item.

Elf hero. Might 2, Wisdom 3, Agility 2, Spirit 4, Wit 3. Damage limit 4, Fear limit 6. Inspiration limit 5. Recommended starting role: Musician. Once per test, after she spends 1 inspiration, a nearby hero gains 1 inspiration.

Dwarf hero. Might 4, Wisdom 2, Agility 2, Spirit 3, Wit 2. Damage limit 6, Fear limit 4. Inspiration limit 4. Recommended starting role: Guardian. After he attacks, he may place one card from the test on top of his deck.

Elf hero. Might 2, Wisdom 3, Agility 4, Spirit 2, Wit 3. Damage limit 4, Fear limit 6. Inspiration limit 5. Recommended starting role: Hunter. Once per round, during his turn, he may spend 1 inspiration to move 1 space without provoking attacks.
Role that evades enemies and is self-sufficient. Burglar skill cards focus on stealth, evasion, and solo capability. Recommended starting hero: Bilbo.
Role that helps the party prepare for any situation. Captain skill cards support team readiness and coordination. Recommended starting hero: Aragorn.
Role that protects others and has strong defenses. Guardian skill cards focus on absorbing damage and shielding allies. Recommended starting hero: Gimli.
Role that attacks for heavy damage. Hunter skill cards focus on maximizing hits and attack efficiency. Recommended starting hero: Legolas.
Role that inspires others to succeed at their tasks. Musician skill cards boost test outcomes and provide support abilities. Recommended starting hero: Elena.
Role that helps the party move quickly. Pathfinder skill cards focus on movement, exploration, and positioning. Recommended starting hero: Beravor.
One of the five hero stats. Represents physical strength. Used for melee weapon attacks, negating damage, and strength-related tests. Shown as a fist icon.
One of the five hero stats. Represents knowledge and perception. Used for some weapon attacks, lore-related tests, and magical effects. Shown as an eye icon.
One of the five hero stats. Represents dexterity and nimbleness. Used for ranged attacks, evasion tests, and movement-related checks. Shown as a feather icon.
One of the five hero stats. Represents willpower and courage. Used for resisting fear, inspiring allies, and magical attacks. Shown as a sun icon.
One of the five hero stats. Represents cunning and cleverness. Used for interact tests, deception, and creative problem-solving. Shown as a diamond icon.

Icon found on some skill cards in the upper-left corner. Each success icon on a card revealed during a test provides one success. Successes are spent to resolve item abilities during attacks, or counted to pass/fail skill tests.
Icon found on some skill cards in the upper-left corner. Has no inherent effect during tests, but each fate icon can be converted to one success by spending one inspiration token.

Icon on item cards and enemy portraits. A hero with a ranged weapon can attack enemies in adjacent spaces (not just their own space). Enemies with the ranged icon can attack heroes in adjacent spaces.
Keyword on skill cards: Sprint X. During your turn, you can discard a card with this keyword to move X additional spaces. You can perform actions between each movement.
Keyword on skill cards: Strike X. During your attack test, you may discard a card with this keyword to add X hits to the attack.
Keyword on skill cards: Guard X. When you or a hero in your space would suffer damage or fear, you may discard a card with this keyword to prevent any combination of X damage and fear.
Keyword on skill cards: Rest X. At the end of your turn, you can discard a card with this keyword to discard any combination of X of your face-down damage or fear cards.
Keyword on skill cards. After performing a test, you can discard a card with this keyword to gain a Hidden boon card from the supply.
Keyword: Scout X. Reveal X cards from the top of your skill deck. You may prepare one of them (place face-up below hero card). Place remaining cards on the top or bottom of your deck in any order.
Attack modifier. This attack ignores the enemy's armor, applying hits directly to health (after sorcery, if any).
Attack modifier. This attack ignores the enemy's sorcery, applying hits directly to health (after armor, if any).
Attack modifier. This attack permanently reduces the enemy's armor by one before hits are applied.
Attack modifier. Each enemy figure in the group suffers the full number of hits from this attack, rather than hits being applied to one figure at a time.
Attack modifier. If this attack reduces the enemy's current health by at least half, the enemy is defeated regardless of remaining health.
Attack modifier. This attack exhausts the enemy group. If the group is elite, stun also prevents it from counterattacking this attack.
Boon card. If you are attacked, before you test, prevent all damage and fear from that attack and discard this boon. When you attack, you may discard this boon to add 1 hit.
Boon card. At the end of your turn, if there are no nearby enemies, discard this boon. If you suffer damage or fear, discard this boon. When you attack, add 2 hits.
Boon card. When you test, you may discard this boon to discard any number of cards from the test and reveal that many additional cards from your deck as replacements.

Physical harm suffered by heroes. When a hero suffers damage, they reveal a card from the 28-card damage deck. Face-up damage cards have persistent negative effects. Total damage (face-up and face-down) reaching the hero's damage limit triggers a last stand.

Psychological harm suffered by heroes. When a hero suffers fear, they reveal a card from the 28-card fear deck. Face-up fear cards have persistent negative effects. Total fear reaching the hero's fear limit triggers a last stand.

The maximum amount of damage (face-up and face-down combined) a hero can have before triggering a last stand. Printed on the hero card. Varies by hero: Bilbo has 3, Elena and Legolas have 4, Aragorn, Beravor, and Gimli have 5 or 6.

The maximum amount of fear (face-up and face-down combined) a hero can have before triggering a last stand. Printed on the hero card. Varies by hero: Gimli has 4, Aragorn and Beravor have 5, Elena and Legolas have 6, Bilbo has 7.

When a hero's total damage or fear reaches their limit, they must perform a last stand via the app's party button. The app presents a test; failing means the hero is defeated and removed from the board. The party loses the adventure at the next shadow phase unless the final objective is completed.

Some effects specifically inflict face-down damage or fear. These cards are placed without reading their text, avoiding persistent negative effects. Face-down cards still count toward the hero's limit. They can be removed by the Rest keyword.

A type of skill card with no success or fate icons and no useful abilities. Each hero starts with one weakness in their 15-card skill deck. Additional weaknesses may be gained during adventures. Their purpose is to clutter your deck, reducing test effectiveness. Can be prepared to temporarily remove from deck.

A special type of skill card reflecting a hero's memorable choices during a campaign. Title cards usually have powerful abilities usable only once per adventure. When gained, they are prepared immediately.

One of 6 basic skill cards (numbered 1-6) included in every hero's deck. Each has a keyword ability and may have success or fate icons. There are 30 basic cards in the box (6 unique, duplicated across heroes).
Skill cards specific to each hero, numbered 1-5 with the hero's name on the bottom. 30 hero skill cards total in the box (5 per hero). These reflect each hero's unique abilities and lore.

Skill cards specific to each role, numbered 1-3 with the role name on the bottom. Green-colored text boxes for easy identification. 72 role skill cards total in the box. Each hero takes 3 matching their current role.

A skill card placed face-up below a hero card. Its abilities are active and can be used as described. Maximum of 4 prepared cards per hero. Prepared cards are not shuffled into the deck during resets.

The first phase of each round. All heroes take turns (in chosen order) performing two actions each. Actions are Travel, Attack, and Interact. Ends when all heroes have gone and the hourglass button is selected.

The second phase of each round. Consists of three steps: Enemy Activation (enemies move and attack), Darkness (heroes in darkness suffer fear), and Threat (threat bar increases). The app guides all steps.
The third phase of each round. Heroes reset their skill decks (shuffle discard pile back in) and Scout 2 (reveal 2 cards, may prepare 1). After scouting, a new round begins.
Hero action. Move up to two spaces (each move = one adjacent space). Can interrupt movement to perform second action between moves. Moving out of a readied enemy's space provokes an attack.
Hero action. Choose an item with a stat icon and an enemy in range. Perform an attack test, spend successes to resolve item abilities (hits and modifiers). Each ability resolves once per attack.

Hero action. Interact with a search, person, or threat token in your space. Select the token and action button in the app; follow instructions. Progresses objectives, finds items, neutralizes threats.

Placed on unexplored map tiles. When a hero enters any space on an unexplored tile, they must explore (not a separate action). Confirm in app to discard token and gain 1 inspiration. Double-sided with inspiration.
Placed on hero cards. Spent during tests to convert 1 fate icon to 1 success. Gained from exploring tiles, card abilities, and game effects. Each hero has an inspiration limit on their hero card.
A point-of-interest token on the map. Heroes perform an interact action to investigate. Often leads to discovering items, gaining lore, or progressing objectives. Double-sided with threat tokens.
Represents NPCs on the map. Heroes interact with them for story events, quests, and information. 8 person tokens in 4 variations included in the box.
Represents dangers on the map. Each threat token adds +1 to threat increase per round. Heroes can interact with them to remove or resolve them. Double-sided with search tokens.
Placed on map spaces. Heroes in a space with a darkness token are in darkness and suffer fear during the darkness step. 5 darkness tokens included in the box.

Placed on trinket item cards. When instructed to deplete a trinket, spend one depletion token. When all tokens are spent, the trinket flips face-down and cannot be used for the rest of the adventure. Trinkets reset at the start of each adventure. 21 depletion tokens in the box.
Tokens with plastic stands used to identify duplicate or elite enemy groups on the map. 12 enemy banners included. Elite groups use red-background banners matching their app portrait icon.
One or more plastic figures of the same enemy type that move and attack together. Each group has a portrait in the app showing its stats. Groups become exhausted after activating or counterattacking.
A stronger enemy group with a spiked portrait frame and banner icon. Has special bonuses listed in its enemy menu. Unlike standard enemies, elite enemies can counterattack even when exhausted.

A defensive value on some enemies. Hits reduce armor before health. If an enemy is not defeated, it regains all armor at the end of the attack. Pierce ignores armor; Sunder permanently reduces it by 1.
A defensive value on some enemies (similar to armor but magical). Hits reduce sorcery before health. If an enemy is not defeated, it regains all sorcery. Smite ignores sorcery.
An enemy group with a bright (non-darkened) portrait in the app. Readied enemies can activate, counterattack, and be provoked. Heroes moving out of a readied enemy's space must provoke it first.

An enemy group with a darkened portrait in the app. Exhausted enemies cannot activate, counterattack, or be provoked (exception: elite enemies can still counterattack). All enemies are readied at the end of the shadow phase.

After a hero attacks an enemy and does not defeat it, the enemy may counterattack if it is in range and not exhausted (elite enemies ignore the exhaustion requirement). The hero selects Yes in the app and resolves the enemy attack.

When a hero moves out of a readied enemy's space or interacts with a token in its space, the enemy is provoked and attacks the hero first. Select the provoke button in the app, then the enemy portrait. Provoking does not exhaust the enemy.

Displayed at the top of the app screen. Fills as threat increases each round (+2 per hero, +1 per threat token, +1 per unexplored tile). Thresholds trigger events. If completely full, the heroes lose the adventure.

Marked positions on the threat bar. When threat reaches a threshold, a threat event activates. Each threshold shows the amount needed to trigger it. Heroes can preview events by selecting thresholds in the app.

Displayed below the threat bar in the app. Heroes must complete a series of objectives to win the adventure. Completing one objective automatically provides the next. The final objective must be completed before the threat bar fills.
Shown in the app around placed map tiles. Indicates where the map will expand as heroes explore and progress. Helps players leave physical room on the table for new tiles.
Located in the lower-left corner of the app screen. Used to access hero information and to trigger last stands when a hero's damage or fear limit is reached.
Located in the lower-right corner of the app screen. Selected after all heroes have taken their turns to end the action phase and proceed to the shadow phase.

Standard map tiles used during adventures. 22 journey map tiles in the box. Each has a unique alphanumeric code for identification and orientation. Divided into one or more spaces enclosed by gray borders.
Special larger map tiles used in battle encounters. 2 battle map tiles included. Not used in the first adventure; introduced in later adventures per the Rules Reference advanced rules.

Tokens representing physical features on battle maps. Types include: 4 fire pit/statue, 9 bush/boulder, 4 barrels/table, 10 stream/wall, and 3 pit/mist. Not used in the first adventure. 10 terrain cards also included.

Cards representing equipment heroes carry. 83 item cards total in the box. Types include one-handed items, two-handed items, armor, and trinkets. Items have stat icons for attacks and abilities that spend successes.

A special type of item card with the trinket icon along the bottom. Trinkets have a starting number of depletion tokens. Depleting a trinket spends one token; when all are spent, the trinket flips face-down. Trinkets reset at the start of each adventure.

A hero can attack with more than one item if all chosen items share the stat being tested. Successes can be spent on abilities from any of the chosen items. If attacking an adjacent enemy, all items must have the ranged icon.

A component is nearby another if they share the same space or are in adjacent spaces. A nearby space refers to that space and all adjacent spaces. A component is not nearby itself. Many abilities and effects reference nearby.

Some abilities 'apply' hits and modifiers to enemies without being attacks. The hero inputs hits and modifiers in the enemy menu and selects Apply, but always selects No if prompted for a counterattack.
Italicized text on skill and item cards (e.g., Dwarf, Tactic, Song, Knowledge, Innate, Valour). Traits have no inherent effect but are referenced by other game effects.
A resource tracked in the app, earned during adventures. Spent between adventures to upgrade item cards to higher tiers. Can be ignored during the first adventure.
A resource tracked in the app, earned during adventures. Spent between adventures to purchase new skill cards for hero decks. Can be ignored during the first adventure.

A series of linked adventures that tell an ongoing story. Heroes and progress carry over between adventures. The recommended first campaign is Bones of Arnor. The campaign saves in the app between sessions.
The recommended starting campaign included with the base game. A multi-adventure story set in the ruins of the ancient kingdom of Arnor. Normal difficulty is recommended for first-time players.
